scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This is Session 1 of a two-session set of interviews conducted as part of Bryan Waterman's exiting NYUAD.

Note: there are two recordings in this first session; the first recording is the bulk of the interview, and then a 4-minute epilogue was added to describe Bryan's Zoom background, which is an image of artwork that was special to him.
[summary in process]

did.unittitle: Interview session with Deborah Williams and Lauren Kata (01:49:23)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Deborah William - Clinical Full Professor at the School of Liberal Studies of New York University and facilitated by Lauren Kata - Assistant Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project.

In this interview, NYU affiliated faculty member Deborah Williams of the Literature and Creative Writing program delves into various aspects of her experience at NYU Abu Dhabi since the days of the Downtown campus. She highlighs student engagement and the evolution of the curriculum over time, as well as her involvement in initiating literary journals and community reading events. Williams also reflects on the residential aspect of campus life, sharing personal anecdotes about living accommodations and community dynamics. Additionally, she addresses pressing issues such as diversity, labor rights, and the quality of discourse within the institution, emphasizing the need for meaningful conversations and systemic change. Despite facing institutional challenges, Williams expresses appreciation for the extraordinary students and colleagues she has encountered at NYU Abu Dhabi.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Justin Stearns (Faculty Labor Committee) and Lauren Kata (01:47:36)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: [summary in process]

did.unittitle: Interview session with Sebastian Grube and Lauren Kata (01:06:51)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session, with Sebastian Grube, former Community Engagement and Marketing Officer at NYU Abu Dhabi Art Gallery, recorded on Friday, June 24, 2022, and facilitated by Lauren Kata, Assistant Librarian for Archives and Special Collections, NYUAD Library. Conducted as part of The Memory Project.

In this session, Sebastian Grube shares his experiences at NYU Abu Dhabi as a member of the Class of 2018 and a former Community Engagement and Marketing Officer at NYUAD Art Gallery. Grube shares how he experienced Candidate Weekend at Sama Tower and witnessed the Saadiyat campus construction. He also shares that interacting with upper class students who had lived in Sama Tower enabled him to get to know and navigate around Abu Dhabi better. The multicultural setting and academic interdisciplinarity offered at NYUAD changed Grube's perspective on what it means to be a global leader and assisted him throughout his journey as a Music and Theater program double major student, and as Art Gallery Community Engagement and Marketing officer. Grube is now looking to continue his journey as an artist who brings artistic sensibilities into business context

did.unittitle: Interview session with Judi Olson (Theater Program) and Lauren Kata (01:44:44)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Judi Olson, Instructor of Costume Design and Arts and Humanities Representative on the Academic Staff Committee of Staff Council at NYUAD, recorded on Monday, July 4th, 2022, and facilitated by Lauren Kata, Assistant Librarian for Archives and Special Collections, NYUAD Library. Conducted as part of The Memory Project.

Judi Olson graduated from NYU's Tisch School of the Arts in 2009 and joined NYU Abu Dhabi in 2012. Between 2012 and 2016, Olson worked part-time on theater productions and joined NYUAD full-time in 2016. During her first four years, she worked on multiple student productions such as The Ramayana, The Aeneid, A Dream Play, and The Odyssey. In this interview session, Olson talks about the fluidity of the theater program and the transient nature of NYUAD, as well as the challenges and accomplishments of the past six years. Olson also shares nostalgic memories of the brunch culture in Abu Dhabi as a tradition of the expat community in the UAE, and her participation in the Abu Dhabi Choral Group.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Jennifer McGlennon (Early Graduate Programs) and Lauren Kata (01:51:11)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Jennifer McGlennon, Former Vice Provost of NYUAD Graduate Programs, facilitated by Lauren Kata, Assistant Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project.

Jennifer McGlennon provides a comprehensive overview of her role as the founding Assistant Vice Provost of Graduate Programs at NYU Abu Dhabi. She shares her background in academia and consulting, including her involvement in educational projects across Indonesia, Australia, and the UAE. McGlennon reflects on the cultural shifts she witnessed in the UAE, particularly in Dubai, where she previously spearheaded educational initiatives. The interview delves into the complexities of establishing graduate programs at NYUAD, highlighting negotiations with department chairs in New York and the challenges of harmonizing institutional cultures across NYU's diverse schools. McGlennon emphasizes the importance of aligning resources and policies to support the needs of PhD students and faculty members, navigating funding arrangements and support services for international scholars. Throughout the discussion, McGlennon underscores the collaborative efforts between NYUAD and stakeholders, including the UAE government and NYU's central administration. She discusses the role of strategic research projects in shaping academic offerings and fostering interdisciplinary collaborations, concluding by reflecting on NYUAD's evolution into a premier academic institution in the region.

did.unittitle: Office of Social Responsibility - Partner Interviews, OSR 10th Anniversary Project

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Twenty-one (21) audio recorded interviews conducted and recorded by PPPT researcher Laura Assanmal during the summer of 2021, she was a team member in the NYUAD Office of Social Responsibility, the project was part of a 10th anniversary reflection on contract colleagues who have been part of the NYUAD community for a decade or longer, and led to the editing and writing of a 10th anniversary OSR booklet.

processinfo.head: Processing Information
processinfo.p: OSR and ASC (Archives and Special Collections)/Memory Project team developed a partner workflow that included the OSR team managing the outreach, interviewing, and consent process for the narrators, and ASC/MP team managing the ingest, summarizing, and accessioning of the audio recordings.
The interviews were conducted by NYUAD Class of 2021 graduate Laura Assanmal, as part of her PPTP summer work with the OSR.
Archives and Special Collections Memory Project summer student assistants Yogesh Dhungana, Stefan Mitikj, and Aiganym Khamitkhanova prepared initial interview summaries for the archivist.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Hazel Lorena and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (38:38)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Hazel Lorena, recorded on [June 10, 2021] and facilitated by Laura Assanmal. Conducted as part of The Memory Project and as part of the Office of Social Responsibility's 10th Anniversary Book Project. The interview was conducted in-person. For the most part, there are only two people present at the interview site (the interviewer and interviewee), but at certain times, there are people passing by in the background.

In this interview session, Hazel Lorena shares her experiences and talks about the happiness brought by working abroad and at NYUAD, as well as about the challenges she faces. Hazel Lorena has been working at NYUAD since August, 2010 as a Public Safety Officer via G4S. Before coming to NYUAD, she lived in Manila, Philippines. She obtained her Bachelor's degree in Hotel and Restaurant Management, and upon graduation, she worked at Unilab, a pharmaceutical company in the Philippines. She later joined an agency in order to work abroad. She was 23 when she came to work in the Middle East and at the time of the interview had been working for 19 years. Lorena has previously worked as a receptionist at Montgomerie Golf Club in Dubai, as a waitress at a restaurant in Qatar, and later joined the security team at NYUAD. She shares her memories of the first class of NYUAD at Sama Tower and adult education classes offered by the Office of Social Responsibility. Despite the challenges, Hazel Lorena is very glad to be a member of the NYUAD community and regards NYU as one big family.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Serkalem Bekele Admasse and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (14:55)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded Memory Project session with Public Safety (G4S) officer, Serkalem Bekele, recorded on the 8th of June, 2021, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, a PPTP student for the Office of Social Responsibility and conducted as part of OSR's 10-year project and The Memory Project. The interview was conducted in-person. For the most part, there are only two people present at the interview site (the interviewer and interviewee), but at certain times, there are people passing by in the background.

In this interview, Serkalem Bekele, Public Safety (G4S) officer, shares her family story, work life, and her journey to NYUAD. She starts by talking about her home (Ethiopia), family, languages she speaks (Amharic, English, Arabic) and her education background (Computer Engineering). Then, she shares about her first arrival to the UAE back in 2005. She mentions about her jobs in different places like Dubai Mall, Abu Dhabi University, the British Embassy and finally in NYUAD. Serkalem also talks about the changes she has seen at NYUAD from a small downtown Sama Tower campus to a huge Saadiyat campus with growing numbers of students and staff over the years. She also added motivating words that during difficult times we should not only focus on problems but on solutions and resolve them. In the end, she appreciates the working environment and the opportunities (language and life-skills courses) provided by OSR for their holistic development.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Fathia Omar and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (17:23)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Fathia Omar, Controlling Supervisor, recorded on Tuesday, June 8, 2021, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 Year Project/Memory Project Partnership. The interview took place on the ground floor of C2, in front of the Office for Social Responsibility in NYU Abu Dhabi. There are two people present at the interview site - the interviewer and interviewee. There is slight background noise at 03:35, perhaps from Fathia's co-workers.

Fathia describes her work in the camera control room and the role of a supervisor in the Security Office; her origin story of Berbera, Somalia, and the UAE; her language skills: Somali, English, Arabic, and her education, which was in UAE government schools, allowing her to get immersed in the Arabic culture. Regarding her journey to NYU Abu Dhabi — a friend told her that G4S is hiring, and she started in Al Ain, but then got invited to join NYUAD in 2010, and got interviewed by Mr. Robert Titus and Ms. Norca Vincent, getting accepted in the end. She wanted to work at NYUAD because she wanted to become independent and responsible — the support from her family helped her along the way. What she likes about the NYUAD community is that she met wonderful, diverse people, but a challenge has been the separation from her family. Lastly, Fathia reflects on possible plans for the future, which for her are studying in medical school to become a phlebotomist.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Yasir Ur Rehman and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (22:29)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded Memory Project session with Yasir Ur Rehman, Control Room Operator in Public Safety Department, recorded on the 9th of June, 2021, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, a PPTP student for the Office of Social Responsibility and conducted as part of OSR's 10-year project and The Memory Project. The interview was recorded in-person on campus. It took place in the C2 building, outside the OSR's office.

In this interview, Yasir Ur Rehman, Control Room Operator in Public Safety, shares his family background, life events, work life, and his journey to NYUAD. He starts by talking about his home in Islamabad, Pakistan, languages he speaks (Pothwari, Urdu, English, Arabic), and his education progression. He talks about his clerical job in the Ministry of Education (Islamabad). During that time, he was influenced by his cousin, so he applied for a G4S visa and moved to the UAE in 2005. He tells of his early struggles at his new workplace with long working hours and low salary, and poor accommodation. After a year, he returned to Pakistan for marriage and again came back to UAE in May 2008. One day, he heard about NYUAD, so he decided to apply and later got accepted. He marks it as one of the most successful events of his life. Yasir also explains about his day-to-day work at NYUAD- handling the public safety control room. Yasir happily shares about his family responsibility, love for his daughter, and expectations from his son. He repeatedly appreciates the caring and humble working environment and management of NYUAD.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Jayaprakash Subramanian and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (11:35)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Jayaprakash Subramanian, Security Officer, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 Year Project/Memory Project Partnership. The location of the interview is C2 building, in front of the Office of Social Responsibility, NYUAD Saadiyat Campus.

Jayaprakash describes his home in India, and how he comes from a farmer's family; he speaks Hindi and English. In 2008, he joined G4S, then two years later the company sent him on an interview at NYU Abu Dhabi, and he got the job in August, 2010. Before NYUAD, he had multiple jobs, working in New Delhi at a bakery, and an advertising agency. In his free time, he likes to cook, watch movies, and meet up with friends (although due to COVID he is limited in that regard). He has taken almost all OSR courses: Public Speaking, Intermediate English, Creative Writing, Excel Crash Course...He likes that he has become financially independent throughout his time at NYUAD, but he misses his family. He is looking forward to his daughter's graduation from high school and a continuation of a stable, secure life.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Andrew Moriasi and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (21:10)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Andrew Moriasi, Security Officer, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership. The location of the interview is C2 building, in front of the Office of Social Responsibility, NYUAD Saadiyat Campus.

Andrew came to the UAE in 2007 and found work as a Security Officer, but then shortly after got the opportunity to do an interview with NYU Abu Dhabi and got the job as a Security Officer, where he received training, but he did additional studying on his own - he went on Coursera and got certified in First Aid and Mental First Aid, as a way to help his friends in crisis situations. He received certification in Religious Tolerance as well. Andrew reveals that he obtained a degree in Food Science in his home country -- Kenya -- and managed to teach his colleagues how to cook.

He discusses how NYUAD allowed him to become financially stable and contribute to his children's college education and that he manages to call them every day to hear how they are doing. When asked, he tells Laura about the OSR courses. Then, Andrew shares that he had COVID in April, 2020, and how he felt during that time (fear of the unknown, solace in friendship) and that he managed to recover in about two weeks. Lastly, he shares what he likes most about working at NYUAD.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Lilia Espanola Francisco and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (23:01)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Lilia Espanola Francisco, recorded on Thursday, June 10th, 2021 and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility NYUAD 10 Years book project, in partnership with The Memory Project.

Lilia Francisco has been working at NYUAD as a Security guard with the Public Safety team for ten years. She is from San Mariano, the Philippines. After their family business went bankrupt, Lilia decided to work abroad, leaving behind her husband and four children. Initially, she was supposed to work in Afghanistan, however upon her arrival to the UAE in order to cross the border, she learned about a vacancy at NYUAD. Without prior experience of working in Public Safety, she completed the necessary training and participated in adult education courses - mainly in English language and IELTS preparatory courses. While it is difficult to be far away from her family, Lilia is working tirelessly to ensure a good quality of life for her children.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Thomas Mathew Kulathingal and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (23:47)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Thomas Mathew Kulathingal, Public Safety Officer, recorded on Thursday, June 10th, 2021 and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, from the Office of Social Responsibility. Conducted as part of The Memory Project and as part of the Office of Social Responsibility's 10th Anniversary Book Project.

Thomas Mathew Kulathingal has started his 12th year at NYUAD as a Public Safety Officer. He is originally from Kerala, in the southern part of India, but he has been in Abu Dhabi since 2006. Thomas Mathew Kulathingal speaks Malayalam, Hindi, Tamil, and a little bit of Kanera language, as well as English. Back in Kerala, he completed a pre-degree program and continued his studies in a technical institute, and worked in agricultural business. After working in Kerala, he came to Abu Dhabi in November, 2006. He saw the NYUAD building in downtown Abu Dhabi while it was still in construction, and later sent his CV and got invited for an interview. During his time at NYUAD, Thomas Kulathingal took classes on Financial literacy, PowerPoint, Excel, Theatre, Photography, and Public speaking. While the pandemic has impacted the way the staff spend their leisure time and visit their families back home, Thomas Mathew Kulathingal also uses his free time to talk to his family and try out new recipes.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Kavitha Karunakarakurup and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (13:06)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio interview session with Kavitha Karunakarakurup, Public Safety (G4S) officer, recorded on Tuesday, 15th June, 2021, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, a PPTP student for the Office of Social Responsibility(OSR). Conducted as part of the OSR's 10 Years at NYUAD Book Project, in partnership with The Memory Project. The interview was recorded in-person on campus. It took place in the C2 building, outside the OSR's office.

in Kerala, India, languages she speaks (Malayalam, Hindi,and English), and her education progression (B.A. in history). Kavita reflects on her work as a stenographer back in New Delhi for 8 years. Next, she speaks about her initial struggle at different workplaces (MENA Building, Emirates Palace) after moving to the UAE. She shares how glad she was to get a job offer at NYUAD. She also describes her role working as Public Safety Officer on the highline. She talks about the significance of the English language course that she took (offered by the OSR). She mentions that she's been financially independent and has built a house while looking after the family. Finally, she reveals her interest in gardening and how she grows vegetables and plants.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Sonu Thomas and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (11:01)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Sonu Thomas, Light Driver for Global Wings, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership. The location of the interview is The Welcome Center of NYUAD, Saadiyat Campus. There are two people present at the interview site - the interviewer and interviewee. There is a short, loud thud at 02:40. At 09:05, Sonu looks for a picture of the house he built in India to show Laura. At 09:51-10:06, Sonu looks up his wife's Youtube channel and Facebook page, and at 10:07 he plays the song for Laura. At 10:35, there is another short, loud thud.

Sonu Thomas is from Kerala, India, and before NYUAD he was working in Delhi in an electronics company. He came to Dubai, UAE in 2003, after his friends told him about the possibilities there, and then in 2005 he took a vacation to get married in India. But he returned with his wife immediately to Dubai, and in 2007 he moved to Abu Dhabi, where his wife got the job of a professional Malayalee singer (her main occupation is a nurse at Corniche Hospital). His two daughters are studying in Abu Dhabi. Throughout working for Abu Dhabi, he changed employment in three different companies related to driving. He got accepted to work for NYUAD at the very beginning, when there were only two drivers. He has taken two OSR courses: English and Arabic. He is satisfied with his NYUAD job, and it has allowed him and his family to lead a comfortable life. He built a home for his parents back in India.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Abdul Jaleel and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (05:52)

dao.xlink:title: Interview session with Abdul Jaleel and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (05:52)

daodesc.p: Interview session with Abdul Jaleel and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (05:52)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Abdul Jaleel, Bus Driver, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership.

In this interview session, Abdul talks about his origin - he is from the village of Kerala, India, and he came directly from there to Abu Dhabi. He speaks Malayalam. Before working at NYUAD, he was working at a maintenance company in Abu Dhabi. He transferred from Global Wings to NYUAD. His kids are back in Kerala, studying, and he sees them whenever he visits. At NYUAD, he drives the shuttle from campus to Yas Mall and Al Wadha. He took some OSR classes at DTC, when NYUAD was at the Downtown Campus. With the salary from NYUAD, he was able to build a home back in Kerala and help his children pursue a better education.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Tara Gurung and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (15:48)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Tara Gurung, Public Safety Officer, recorded on Monday, June 21st, 2021, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, Office of Social Responsibility PPTP Student. Conducted as part of The Memory Project and the 10th Anniversary Book Project. The interview takes place outside the Office of Social Responsibility office, on the Ground floor of NYUAD campus on Saadiyat Island.

Tara Gurung is a Public Safety Officer that specializes in Access Control and Customer Service. She is originally from Itahari area, Nepal, 8 hours away from the capital city of Kathmandu. In addition to English, Tara speaks Nepali, Hindi, and a bit of Arabic. Prior to joining NYUAD, she worked as a primary school teacher back in Nepal. Tara joined G4S and arrived in the UAE on April 16, 2008, and she joined NYUAD as a Public Safety Officer on March 6, 2010. During her time here, Tara participated in workshops provided by the Office of Social Responsibility on computer literacy and cooking. In this session, Tara also shares her memories about Sama Tower. She dedicates her free time to keeping in touch with her family, and hopes to go back to Nepal in the future and be with them.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Mohammed Hoque and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (37:37)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Mohammed Hoque, Worker at ADNH, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership. The interview takes place outside of the Office of Social Responsibility, Ground floor of C2, NYUAD, Saadiyat Campus. There are two people present at the interview site - the interviewer and interviewee. At 14:37, 16:45, 17:06, 17:43, 24:15, several people are talking in the background. At 17:49, there is a quick, sharp sound, perhaps from a microphone. At 31:32, 31:43, some people are passing by with a cleaning cart.

Mohammed Haque is from Lakshmipur District, near Meghna River, Bangladesh. He was working in Saudi Arabia, for Saudi Arabian Airlines, then he joined NYU Abu Dhabi in 2011, and throughout his time at NYUAD, he has worked in multiple positions under ADNH - dining hall and coffee shops at Sama Tower, and even as a Store Clerk in 2014. He has taken English courses at OSR and has attended multiple workshops. He speaks Bengali, English, and Arabic. He worked at the Airlines until 2007, and for a short period of time he went back home to Bangladesh and immediately returned to Abu Dhabi, finding a job at the Abu Dhabi Court. Throughout the interview, he explains Bangladeshi culture and the feeling of responsibility of being the breadwinner. He also states that he talks to his family two to three times a day and that he is able to financially support his wife who is sick, thanking NYUAD for that possibility. He then goes on to describe his job position in Saudi Arabia, opportunities for investment with his friend, and taking care of his family, especially his wife who had to undergo surgery. In the future, he plans to open up a water-based business and he is looking forward to the day when his children study at NYUAD.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Earnie Agramonte Odieres and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (17:30)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Interview session with Earnie Agramonte Odieres, Assistant Chef at NYUAD Dining Department, recorded on Monday, 21st June, 2021, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, a PPTP student for the Office of Social Responsibility(OSR). Conducted as part of the OSR's 10 Years at NYUAD Book Project, in partnership with The Memory Project. The interview took place at the Torch Club (top of D2).

In this interview, Earnie shares about his family, life events, work life, and his journey to NYUAD. He describes his role working as Assistant Chef, planning and preparing the cold and hot kitchen. He shares about his home in Visayas, Philippines, languages he speaks (English, Tagalog, Hiligayhon), and his education progression (Bachelor of Science in Education). Earnie reflects on his work as a high school teacher back in the Philippines for six months before moving to the UAE. Next, he mentions that he was very lucky to have his first job (started as waiter) at NYUAD. Earnie also reveals the difference he has seen from Sama Tower DTC to the larger Saadiyat Campus. He mentions that he's been financially independent and has built a house, bought land, and started a small renting business. Finally, he appreciates the English course he has taken, which was offered by the OSR, and says he loves everyone at NYUAD.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Nadir Khan and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (22:10)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Interview session with Nadir Khan, Shift Supervisor in Public Safety, recorded on Tuesday, 22nd June, 2021, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, a PPTP student for the Office of Social Responsibility(OSR). Conducted as part of the OSR's 10 Years at NYUAD Book Project, in partnership with The Memory Project. The interview was recorded over Zoom.

In this interview, Nadir shares about his family, life events, work life, and his journey to NYUAD. He describes his role working as Shift Supervisor, deploying and looking after the requirements of his colleagues. He speaks about his home in Kashmir, Pakistan, languages he speaks (Urdu, English), and his education progression (Intermediate in Commerce). Nadir reflects on his work in the Pakistani Armed Forces for six years and his resignation. Next, he mentions his first work in the UAE in the Armed Forces Officer Club at Abu Dhabi. He shares how glad he was to get a job offer at NYUAD. Nadir also reveals the difference he has seen from Sama Tower DTC to the larger Saadiyat Campus. He talks about the significance of different language and computer courses that he has taken (offered by the OSR). He mentions that he's been financially independent and has built a house while looking after the family. Finally, he proudly says that he loves every moment spent at NYUAD and hopes to return to his home country in near future.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Lailani Vidal and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (32:19)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Lailani Vidal, Senior Administrator at Serco, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership. The interview takes place outside of the Office of Social Responsibility, on the ground floor of C2, at NYU Abu Dhabi, Saadiyat Island. There are two people present at the interview site - the interviewer and the interviewee. At 24:52-25:58, loud yelling and talking noises from children are noticeable.

Lailani started working for NYUAD in 2010, at the Downtown Campus as an Administrative Assistant for Facility Management under Serco. She explains the difference between soft and hard services in the industry, and she explains her and her colleagues' responsibilities in this job, emphasizing teamwork as crucial for the success of their jobs. She is from the Philippines and she speaks Tagalog, English, and a little bit of Japanese because she worked for a Japanese company back home. She opens up about her struggles obtaining a Bachelor's degree in Industrial Engineering, and how she managed to work in the morning, and study in the afternoon. Then, she reflects on financial struggles and climbing ladders at NYUAD, as well as starting out in the UAE and slowly bringing her family there. She developed a plan for taking care of her kids in agreement with her husband in light of Covid-19, thanks to the flexibility of working from home by Serco. Her intentions for the future include creating a business in the Philippines, probably in the IT sector. She reflects on her time in DTC, and she states that she's taken the English, Arabic, and Excel OSR courses while encouraging other Filipino staff to participate in the programs. She explains that one of her boys will participate in the Boys Education Network, for which Lailani is really excited, and that the other loves spending time at NYUAD. She ends by sharing words of encouragement she tells her oldest son.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Richelle Vallejos and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (10:09)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Richelle Vallejos, Security Officer, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership.

In this interview session, Richelle talks about herself, her family, previous employment, working at NYUAD, and hopes for the future. She is from the Philippines and she is a Security Officer in A1B. She speaks English and Tagalog. She has an undergraduate degree in Computer Security. Her father used to work in the UAE, and he brought her here. With the help of her aunt, who was working in G4S, she managed to get a job there. She came to the UAE, hoping to help out her family, because back home Richelle and her family did not have land or a house, so they stayed with her aunt for about two years. Her father—who worked in a construction company—financially helped them build a house. Then, two out of her three brothers moved to the UAE. She was able to take an English course which is offered by the Office of Social Responsibility. Her plans for the future include moving home to spend some time with her family and potentially finding love and building a family.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Madel De la Cruz and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (15:30)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Madel De la Cruz, CCTV Supervisor, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership. The interview takes place outside of the Office of Social Responsibility, on the ground floor of C2, at NYU Abu Dhabi, Saadiyat Island. There are two people present at the interview site - the interviewer and the interviewee. At 11:45, there is light talking in the background, which slowly by 14:00 starts getting louder.

In this interview session, Madel shares stories of her life before and during working at NYU Abu Dhabi. She starts by saying that she was born and lived close to Manila, Philippines. She speaks Tagalog and English. While in the Philippines, she graduated with a Bachelor's degree in Computer Engineering, and she worked there for about six years, in accounting for a newspaper company rather than her field. Then, a friend told her about a great opportunity for work at G4S in the UAE. Madel was interested in the idea and decided to apply to work for G4S in NYUAD. Throughout the years, she managed to transform her role from a Control Room Operator to a Control Room Supervisor, spanning her career at NYUAD to 11 years and counting. What she especially likes about the working environment there is that even security guards are taken care of, and made to feel comfortable. Madel was a part of the DTC Campus as well, in Sama Tower, and she draws a comparison between the two campuses, saying that Saadiyat is bigger and it's harder to get to know everyone. Then, she reflects on her relationship with her daughter and opens up about a difficult life event with her mother. Madel has taken English and Financial Literacy courses, and Computer classes. She shares some memorable events at NYUAD like Iftar and Staff Appreciation Week and hopes to perhaps move to Canada with her daughter in the future.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Bakht Munir Khan and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (10:08)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Bakht Munir Khan, Security Officer at Public Safety, and facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership.

Bakht is from Nowshera, Pakistan and works at NYU Abu Dhabi's Public Safety Office. He speaks English, Urdu, Pashto, and a little bit of Arabic. Before coming to NYUAD, he spent some time helping his brother with shopkeeping, but he was jobless. Then, he applied to a job position he saw in the newspaper, had an interview in Islamabad, and got accepted to first join the Officers' Club in Abu Dhabi, and then join NYUAD. He wants to spend as much time as he can in Abu Dhabi, giving his three kids the opportunity to receive quality education. Bakht is very connected to his family and takes care of his wife and kids every day. He really likes his position at NYUAD, as he is constantly learning something new from the students and staff. He has taken English classes, and started taking Arabic classes, but unfortunately they got postponed in March 2020. He is grateful for the opportunities to attend workshops and classes. His plan for the future is to give quality education to his kids, just like NYUAD students.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Shihuabudeen and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (13:38)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Shihuabudeen, Light Driver, facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership.

Shihuabudeen is from Kerala, India. He moved to the UAE in 2002, looking for better job opportunities and a better salary. He has switched workplaces, working at one company with his father in the fruits and vegetables district near Souq Mina, then working at another company in Abu Dhabi. Finally he began working at NYU Abu Dhabi. He has four children, the oldest one being in tenth grade. He recalls a memory over the course of 10 years at NYUAD, when he took some students on a trip. For the future, he hopes to bring his family to Abu Dhabi and offer them a better life there.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Jeevan Puthiyaveetil and Laura Assanmal (OSR) (12:50)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Audio-recorded session with Jeevan Puthiyaveetil, Driver at the Transport Department, facilitated by Laura Assanmal, PPTP student. Conducted as part of the Office of Social Responsibility (OSR) 10 year Project/Memory Project Partnership.

Jeevan is from Kerala, India and he really likes the weather there. He works as a driver at the transport department of NYUAD. He has worked for 35 years of his life - spending 10 years in Saudi Arabia as a trailer driver, then in Dubai and Sharjah before moving to a position at NYUAD in 2011 through Global Wings. What made him change different job positions was usually the low salary. He came to the UAE in 1999. He is very thankful to NYUAD for giving him opportunities he never could have imagined - one of which is giving his children quality education. He is proud to say that one son is working in Dubai while the other studies medicine in the Philippines, at a U.S. university. With the help of the NYUAD salary, he was able to build a home back in India. He finished Business English 1 and 2 courses with the Office of Social Responsibility. Some of his dreams for the future are for his youngest son to finish his education and for his eldest to get a job at NYUAD.

did.unittitle: Interviews with NYUAD Community Members [post-Covid]

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: This series of interviews includes interviews conducted after the easing of restrictions due to Covid (after summer 2022). For convenience and comfort, the majority of interviews continued to be conducted via Zoom (remotely). Some interviews, for example during Alumni Reunion Weekends, were conducted in person.
Please read interview summaries in each interviews "Scope and Content" note to understand the scope of what is discussed.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Francis Fourie (Library) and Lauren Kata (01:19:32)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session, with Francis Fourie, NYUAD Library Assistant, recorded on 08/11/2022, and facilitated by Lauren Kata, NYUAD University Archivist. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Francis Fourie reflects on her career in librarianship and her experiences as a member of the New York University Abu Dhabi Library staff. She discusses her professional journey from South Africa to the United States and ultimately to Abu Dhabi, describing how her previous library experience informed her work at NYUAD. Fourie recalls the University's formative years at the Downtown Campus and Sama Tower, reflecting on the transition to the permanent campus on Saadiyat Island and the changes it brought to campus life. Throughout the interview, she emphasizes the close-knit community that characterized NYUAD's early years, highlighting the collegiality, kindness, and collaborative spirit among faculty, staff, and students. She also reflects on supporting students through her work in the Library and as a First Year Dialogue mentor. The interview provides insight into the development of the NYUAD Library and the community culture that shaped the University's early institutional identity.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:02:35–00:07:05 — Professional background and path to NYUAD. Fourie discusses her upbringing in South Africa, work in public libraries, time in the United States, and eventual move to Abu Dhabi.

00:10:20–00:14:15 — First impressions of the Downtown Campus. She recalls interviewing at the Downtown Campus and describes the welcoming atmosphere, close-knit community, and daily life during NYUAD's early years.
00:14:45–00:16:30 — Community garden and shared spaces. Fourie reflects on the Downtown Campus garden and outdoor gathering spaces as important places for building community among faculty and staff.
00:31:20–00:35:10 — First Year Dialogue mentoring. She discusses serving as a mentor for first-year students and reflects on the rewards of supporting students' transition to university life.

00:43:40–00:47:30 — Transition to the Saadiyat campus. Fourie reflects on moving to the permanent campus, comparing the intimacy of the Downtown Campus with the expanded facilities and evolving community on Saadiyat.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Nouf Al Hamly, Rawdha Rashed Almansoori and Lauren Kata (Citizen Researcher) (01:46:38)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Rawdha Rashed Almansoori, Research Outreach, and Nouf Al Hamly, Associate Director of Research Outreach, recorded on 09/12/2022, and facilitated by Lauren Kata NYUAD University Archivist. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Nouf and Rawdha reflect on their work advancing research engagement and public outreach at New York University Abu Dhabi. They discuss the University's evolution from the collaborative atmosphere of the Downtown Campus to the expanded opportunities afforded by the Saadiyat campus, describing how institutional growth strengthened interdisciplinary partnerships and community engagement. The interview explores efforts to make research more accessible through science communication and public programming, including initiatives that incorporate Emirati knowledge and local perspectives into research practice. Al Hamly and Rhodes also discuss the development of the Citizen Researcher program, highlighting its interdisciplinary approach, collaboration with students and faculty, outreach to schools and community organizations, and adaptation during the COVID-19 pandemic. Through examples drawn from both the sciences and the humanities, they emphasize the importance of public participation, collaboration, and inclusive approaches to research that connect academic scholarship with the wider community.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:02:56–00:04:44 — Joining NYUAD and the early Downtown Campus. Nouf describes how she came to NYUAD through conversations with faculty before the opening of the Saadiyat campus, reflecting on the University's early collaborative culture.

00:09:35–00:14:30 — Science communication and the Citizen Researcher program. Rawdha discusses expanding public engagement with research through workshops, community events, and the Citizen Researcher initiative, emphasizing active public participation in research.

00:31:30–00:39:30 (approx.) — Interdisciplinary collaboration and student involvement. The interviewees discuss the role of students, faculty, and research centers in developing Citizen Researcher projects and building collaborations across disciplines.

01:15:08–01:16:45 — Building public trust in research. Nouf Al Hamly reflects on the importance of communicating the scientific process—including uncertainty and failure—and discusses public trust in science during and after the COVID-19 pandemic.

01:36:32–01:38:50 — Digital humanities and community research. Rawdha Rhodes describes a Citizen Researcher project in collaboration with a digital humanities faculty member that invites participants to collect and transcribe restaurant menus from across the UAE to document local food culture.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Bonnie Sutherland (Library) and Lauren Kata (01:48:41)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Bonnie Sutherland, Library Assistant in Acquisitions, recorded on 09/12/2022, and facilitated by Lauren Kata NYUAD University Archivist. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Bonnie Sutherland reflects on her career as a library acquisitions assistant at New York University Abu Dhabi and her journey from Jamaica to Abu Dhabi. She describes her transition from a career in nursing to librarianship through volunteer work at her children's school, eventually joining the NYUAD Library during the University's early years at the Downtown Campus. Sutherland recalls the close-knit atmosphere of the early campus, reflecting on the collaborative relationships among library staff, faculty, and students and the practical challenges of supporting library services in a small, rapidly growing institution. She discusses the transition to the Saadiyat campus, the expansion of library operations, and the evolving needs of the University community. Throughout the interview, Sutherland emphasizes the importance of service, adaptability, and community, illustrating how her work in the Library contributed to the University's growth while fostering meaningful connections with colleagues and students.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:01:45–00:07:30 — Journey from Jamaica to Abu Dhabi. Sutherland discusses relocating with her family to Abu Dhabi, adapting to life in the UAE, and her first impressions of the city.

00:07:30–00:12:30 — Transition from nursing to librarianship. She describes volunteering at her children's school library, discovering a passion for library work, and developing new professional skills.
00:10:54–00:14:40 — Moving to the UAE and cultural adjustment. Bonnie reflects on relocating to Abu Dhabi in 2005 and discusses adapting to different cultural expectations and social norms.
00:15:44–00:17:00 — Children's school experiences. Bonnie reflects on her children's experiences attending school in Abu Dhabi and discusses the challenges of adapting as expatriate students.

00:22:55–00:28:35 — Jamaican community in the UAE and migration. Bonnie describes building a sense of community among Jamaican expatriates and reflects on helping other Jamaican families relocate to the UAE. This extends beyond her personal story and documents a little-known aspect of migration to Abu Dhabi.
00:47:00–00:49:00 — First impressions of the UAE. Researchers interested in expatriate experiences will find this useful, particularly because Bonnie arrived before NYUAD was established.
00:49:10–00:54:00 — Early NYUAD Library and acquisitions
00:59:00–01:00:00 — Early library culture and cross-functional collaboration
01:15:00–01:20:00 — Transition from Downtown Campus to Saadiyat
01:20:00–01:24:00 — Library acquisitions and collection development
01:24:00–01:26:00 — Leisure Collection
01:27:00–01:29:00 — Academic freedom at NYUAD

did.unittitle: Interview Session with Mayada Oudah (Campus Cats) and Lauren Kata (01:22:59)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Mayada Oudah - Medisearch Associate at Social Science Experimental Lab, recorded on 2023-11–6 and facilitated by Lauren Kata - Assistant Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Mayada Oudah reflects on her academic career and research in computer science, artificial intelligence, and human-robot interaction, describing her transition to interdisciplinary research integrating biometrics, virtual reality, and behavioral science. She discusses joining New York University Abu Dhabi and the University's growing research environment before turning to her longstanding involvement in caring for the campus cat population. Oudah recounts the development of NYUAD's Trap-Neuter-Return program, describing how collaboration among students, faculty, staff, veterinarians, and University leadership helped establish a sustainable approach to animal welfare on campus. She reflects on balancing the needs of the cats with those of the campus community, addressing misconceptions about free-roaming cats, and building support for responsible colony management. Throughout the interview, Oudah emphasizes the importance of community engagement, education, and compassion, illustrating how informal grassroots initiatives became an enduring part of campus life.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:02:30–00:08:30 — Research background and interdisciplinary scholarship. Mayada Oudah discusses her academic background in computer science and her interdisciplinary research in artificial intelligence, human-robot interaction, biometrics, virtual reality, and behavioral science.

00:22:05–00:23:01 — Beginning of the campus cat care journey. Oudah recounts how she first became involved in caring for the campus cats, describing her initial concerns for their welfare and how those experiences inspired her to become an advocate for humane animal care.

00:23:25–00:24:00 — Discovering campus cat management protocols. Oudah recalls learning about existing procedures for managing the campus cat population and explains how she began working with others to improve coordination and support for the program.

00:34:20–00:34:39 — Caring for the campus cats during COVID-19. Oudah reflects on the challenges of maintaining care for the campus cat population during the COVID-19 pandemic and the efforts required to ensure their continued well-being.

00:34:00–00:43:00 — Building the Trap-Neuter-Return (TNR) program. Oudah discusses collaborating with students, faculty, staff, veterinarians, and University leadership to establish a sustainable Trap-Neuter-Return program and promote responsible colony management.

00:35:30–00:35:44 — The personal value of cats. Oudah reflects on the important role cats have played in her family life, describing them as beloved members of the household and explaining how that perspective has shaped her commitment to animal welfare.

00:58:00–01:07:30 — Gingerbell and community advocacy. Oudah recounts the rescue of Gingerbell, describing the community effort to protect the cat, secure veterinary care, and ultimately arrange an adoption, illustrating the strong sense of collective responsibility among members of the NYUAD community.

01:06:30–01:12:00 — Institutional support for campus cat management. Oudah reflects on the University's evolving approach to managing the campus cat population, emphasizing collaboration between volunteers and University leadership to balance animal welfare with campus operations.

01:18:00–01:24:00 — Well-known campus cats and community identity. Oudah shares stories about several of the campus's best-known cats, explaining how they became familiar figures within the NYUAD community and contributed to campus culture.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Githmi Rabel, Huma Umar, Sidra Dahhan, and Stefan Mitikj (The Gazelle) and Lauren Kata (01:42:16)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Githmi Rabel - Co-editor in Chief, Huma Umar - Co-editor in Chief, Sidra Dahhan - Managing Editor, Stefan Mitikj - Managing Editor, recorded on 2023-21–05 and facilitated by Lauren Kata - Assistant Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Githmi Rabel, Huma Umar, Sidra Dahhan, and Stefan Mitikj, members of the 2022–2023 editorial team of The Gazelle, reflect on their experiences leading New York University Abu Dhabi's independent student newspaper. They discuss their individual paths from student writers to editors while describing the collaborative process of producing a weekly publication within a diverse international university community. The interview explores The Gazelle's role in amplifying student voices, providing nuanced coverage of local, regional, and global issues, and reporting on topics affecting the Middle East and the Global South that are often underrepresented in mainstream media. The editors also reflect on the responsibilities of student journalism, including maintaining editorial independence, navigating cultural and institutional contexts, balancing accountability with student safety, and making thoughtful editorial decisions. Throughout the interview, they emphasize The Gazelle's commitment to ethical, inclusive journalism and its role in fostering dialogue within the NYUAD community and beyond.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:02:45–00:10:35 — Joining The Gazelle and becoming student editors. Each editor reflects on how they became involved with the newspaper, their progression into editorial roles, and what drew them to student journalism.

00:10:40–00:12:45 — The role of an independent student newspaper. Stefan Mitikj discusses why an independent publication is fundamental to a university community and reflects on The Gazelle's responsibility to hold institutions accountable. This becomes a recurring theme throughout the interview.

00:18:45–00:20:30 — Reporting on the Middle East with local perspective. Githmi Rabel reflects on covering regional issues from within the Middle East, emphasizing the value of nuanced reporting that challenges simplistic or Orientalist narratives.

00:20:30–00:25:00 — Reporting on the Global South. The editors discuss The Gazelle's sustained coverage of the Global South, highlighting student reporting from Iran and other underrepresented regions and reflecting on how the experience broadened their own perspectives.

00:28:10–00:29:30 — Editorial decisions and student safety. Huma Umar discusses adapting language and editorial practices to protect student contributors while maintaining journalistic integrity, particularly when reporting on sensitive topics.

00:32:55–00:33:45 — Censorship, editorial judgment, and responsible reporting. Githmi Rabel reflects on balancing editorial independence with practical considerations when publishing sensitive stories, describing the editorial process of revising articles while preserving their essential message.

did.unittitle: Interview session with (Al Diwan SIG) Mohammed Muqbel, Roudha Almarzouqui, Aisha Alhammadi, Fatima Al Breiki, Mohammad Amjed, Shamma AlMansoori and Lauren Kata (01:50:35)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: In this oral history interview, Mohammed Muqbel, Roudha Almarzouqui, Aisha Alhammadi, Fatima Al Breiki, Mohammad Amjed, and Shamma AlMansoori reflect on their experiences leading Al Diwan, New York University Abu Dhabi's student interest group dedicated to Arabic poetry and literature. They discuss founding the organization to create a welcoming space for students to engage with Arabic literary traditions and cultural expression within the University's diverse international community. The interview explores the significance of Arabic poetry in fostering cultural identity while emphasizing that curiosity and enthusiasm, rather than language proficiency, are the primary requirements for participation. The members also describe efforts to expand the group's activities through discussions of Arabic literature, public poetry readings, and creative writing workshops that encourage students to compose and share their own work. Throughout the interview, they emphasize community, inclusivity, and the importance of sustaining Arabic literary traditions while creating opportunities for cultural exchange at NYUAD.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:05:20–00:08:15 — Founding Al Diwan. Aisha Alhammadi discusses recognizing the absence of programming centered on Arabic literature and explains the motivation for establishing Al Diwan as a student interest group.

00:14:40–00:19:15 — Why Arabic poetry matters. The members reflect on Arabic poetry as a means of cultural expression, discussing its linguistic richness and its role in understanding history, identity, and shared cultural traditions.

00:24:45–00:30:15 — Inclusivity and language proficiency. The group explains that Al Diwan welcomes students of all levels of Arabic proficiency, emphasizing curiosity, appreciation, and a willingness to learn over linguistic expertise.

00:35:10–00:35:30 — Curiosity over expertise. Fatima Al Breiki explains that resources for learning Arabic literature already exist, but Al Diwan seeks to build a community of students motivated by curiosity and passion rather than prior knowledge.

00:39:20–00:41:10 — Arabic identity within an international university. Aisha Alhammadi reflects on the importance of creating a space that celebrates Arabic language and culture within NYUAD's multicultural student body, complementing the many other cultural organizations on campus.

00:42:30–00:43:45 — Student poetry and creative writing workshops. Mohammad Amjed discusses plans for poetry-writing workshops and opportunities for students to compose, perform, and share their own original Arabic poetry.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Peter Christensen (CABO) and Lauren Kata (01:52:16)

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Peter Christensen - NYU Abu Dhabi's Chief Administrator and Business Officer and facilitated by Lauren Kata - Assistant Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Peter L. Christensen reflects on his leadership in the governance and administration of New York University Abu Dhabi, discussing how finance, facilities, information technology, procurement, and campus operations support the University's academic mission. He describes NYUAD's distinctive governance structure and the collaborative planning processes between New York University and Tamkeen that guide institutional priorities, budgeting, and long-term development. Christensen reflects on the University's evolution from its entrepreneurial early years into a mature institution with established administrative systems while emphasizing the importance of aligning operational resources with teaching, research, and student success. Throughout the interview, he expresses appreciation for the sustained support provided by Tamkeen and reflects on the culture of optimism, collaboration, and possibility that has characterized NYUAD's development. The interview offers insight into the governance, operational philosophy, and institutional stewardship that have shaped the University's growth.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:01:30–00:06:30 — Defining the academic support portfolio. Christensen explains the scope of his responsibilities, arguing that finance, facilities, procurement, campus safety, and information technology are integral to supporting the University's academic mission.

00:22:30–00:29:30 — Aligning resources with academic priorities. He discusses strategic planning, budgeting, and the importance of ensuring that operational decisions advance teaching, research, and student success.

01:16:45–01:18:45 — From startup to operating institution. Christensen reflects on NYUAD's transition from an entrepreneurial startup environment to a mature university with established administrative systems and operating processes.

01:24:45–01:26:30 — Institutional maturity and administrative growth. He compares the University's early informal culture with the predictability and efficiency that accompanied institutional growth while acknowledging the trade-offs of increased administrative structure.

01:37:40–01:42:30 — Governance model and partnership with Tamkeen. Christensen explains NYUAD's governance structure, the relationship between NYU and Tamkeen, and why he believes the governance agreement has been central to the University's long-term success.

did.unittitle: Interview session with (Core Technology Platform Scientists) James Weston, Matthew O'Connor, Rachid Rezgui and Lauren Kata

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with James Weston, Matthew J O'Connor, Rachid Rezgui facilitated by Lauren Kata - Assistant Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, James Weston, Matthew O'Connor, and Rachid Rezgui reflect on their work as Research Instrumentation Scientists within New York University Abu Dhabi's Core Technology Platform. They discuss the development of the University's shared scientific research infrastructure, describing how the Core Technology Platform provides researchers across multiple disciplines with access to advanced instrumentation and technical expertise. The interview explores the challenges of relocating laboratories and specialized equipment from the Center for Science and Engineering in Musaffah to the Saadiyat campus, as well as adapting laboratory spaces to support a rapidly expanding research enterprise. The interviewees also reflect on the evolution of the Core Technology Platform, emphasizing its collaborative approach to supporting faculty and students, promoting equitable access to research resources, and fostering interdisciplinary scientific discovery. Throughout the discussion, they illustrate how technical expertise, innovation, and teamwork contributed to building NYUAD's research environment during its formative years.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:02:30–00:05:30 — Joining the Core Technology Platform. James Weston, Matthew O'Connor, and Rachid Rezgui describe their professional backgrounds and explain how they came to NYUAD during the University's early years, joining the team responsible for supporting scientific research.

00:04:30–00:09:30 — Moving laboratories and scientific equipment to Saadiyat. The interviewees recount the logistical planning required to relocate specialized laboratory equipment from the Center for Science and Engineering in Musaffah to the new campus while maintaining research continuity.

00:14:50–00:18:30 — Working across multiple early campus locations. They reflect on the relationship between the Downtown Campus, Sama Tower, and the Center for Science and Engineering, describing how the University's distributed facilities shaped collaboration before the move to Saadiyat.

00:43:35–00:45:15 — Retrofitting laboratories on the Saadiyat campus. James Weston discusses adapting architecturally designed laboratory spaces to meet the practical requirements of advanced scientific research, highlighting the challenges of creating functional research environments within a completed building.

00:47:30–00:50:30 — The philosophy of the Core Technology Platform. Rachid Rezgui explains how the CTP differs from traditional laboratory models by providing shared access to sophisticated instrumentation, standardized training, and technical support that enables collaboration across research groups.

00:58:00–01:04:00 — Supporting interdisciplinary research. The interviewees reflect on how the Core Technology Platform has evolved to support an increasingly diverse research community, emphasizing collaboration with faculty and students from multiple scientific disciplines.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Diana Chester (early NYUAD) and Lauren Kata

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Diana Chester, a former academic technician and instructor at early NYUAD, recorded on 2024-05-07 and facilitated by Lauren Kata, Associate Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Diana Chester reflects on her experiences helping build New York University Abu Dhabi from its earliest planning stages, beginning with her work in New York supporting the Abu Dhabi project before the University established its first campus. She describes her evolving career as NYUAD grew, transitioning from academic technology to teaching interactive media while contributing to the development of media laboratories, the Arts Center, and other academic initiatives. Chester recalls the University's formative years at the NYUAD Institute and the Downtown Campus, the move to the Saadiyat campus, and the collaborative culture that characterized the institution's rapid growth. She also reflects on building a personal and professional life in Abu Dhabi, describing how the city, its communities, and her experiences in the UAE influenced both her teaching and research. Throughout the interview, Chester illustrates the adaptability, creativity, and optimism that shaped NYUAD's transformation from an ambitious project into a thriving university.

did.unittitle: Selected index of segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:07:30–00:13:20 — First trip from New York to Abu Dhabi. Chester recalls traveling from New York to Abu Dhabi to establish an early video conference connection between the New York office and the Al Bateen office. She reflects on her first impressions of Abu Dhabi after living in India and describes the city's early landscape.

00:15:30–00:21:35 — The NYUAD Institute and the Regis Al Bateen years. Chester discusses the NYUAD Institute's public programming, the University's early presence in Abu Dhabi, and the collaborative atmosphere before the opening of the Downtown Campus.

00:21:35–00:22:35 — Improvising technical solutions in early Abu Dhabi. Chester recounts searching electronics shops on Electra Street for specialized cables and components needed for Institute events, illustrating the resourcefulness required to support NYUAD's earliest programs before institutional infrastructure was established.

00:22:45–00:27:00 — From project to university. Chester reflects on recognizing the moment when the Abu Dhabi project evolved into a functioning university community with its own identity and long-term future.
00:31:10–00:35:45 — Building a life in Abu Dhabi. Chester discusses getting to know Abu Dhabi, forming friendships, and developing a sense of belonging within the local and expatriate communities.

00:37:10–00:45:00 — Candidate Weekend and Marhaba. Chester recalls the University's earliest recruitment events, welcoming the first cohorts of students, and the community spirit that characterized NYUAD's formative years.

00:53:45–00:56:35 — An evolving career alongside NYUAD. Chester describes how her responsibilities expanded from academic technology into teaching interactive media as the University and its academic programs developed.

00:56:36–01:03:10 — Building media laboratories and the Arts Center. Chester reflects on developing media laboratories, supporting specialized software and equipment for the arts, collaborating with faculty, and helping establish the technological infrastructure that supported creative teaching and research. She also discusses her transition into the Arts and Humanities Division as NYUAD expanded its academic offerings.

01:03:10–01:10:15 — Transition from the Downtown Campus to Saadiyat. Chester reflects on moving to the permanent campus, describing what the early Saadiyat campus looked like and how the transition transformed teaching, creative work, and campus life.

01:15:36–01:23:50 — Identity, community, and belonging in Abu Dhabi. Chester reflects on living in Abu Dhabi as a Jewish and queer woman, discussing community, identity, and the ways her personal experiences shaped her understanding of the city and the University's diverse environment.

01:24:00–01:29:00 — How living in the UAE influenced teaching and research. Chester discusses the ways her experiences in Abu Dhabi informed her research interests, teaching practice, and creative work.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Saba Brelvi (Domestic Workers) and Lauren Kata

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Saba Brelvi, a member of NYUAD Community, recorded on 2024-05-23 and facilitated by Lauren Kata, Associate Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Saba Brelvi reflects on her experiences as the spouse of a New York University Abu Dhabi faculty member and as an active member of the University's residential community. She discusses witnessing NYUAD's growth from its early years at the Downtown Campus through the move to the Saadiyat campus, describing how the close-knit community evolved as the institution expanded. Brelvi recounts her longstanding advocacy for the rights and well-being of domestic workers in the United Arab Emirates, including conducting research, developing educational resources, organizing information sessions, and collaborating with University offices to support faculty and staff in making informed and ethical employment decisions. She also reflects on the importance of recognizing domestic workers as members of the broader campus community and describes her efforts to embed these initiatives within University programs so they would continue beyond her own involvement. The interview offers a distinctive perspective on community life, social responsibility, and institutional development at NYUAD.

did.unittitle: Selected index segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:05:45–00:12:30 — Joining the NYUAD community. Brelvi reflects on moving to Abu Dhabi, her family's connection to NYUAD, and the close-knit residential community that developed during the University's early years.

00:19:30–00:26:00 — Community life at the Downtown Campus and Sama Tower. She describes everyday life in the early residential community, relationships among families, and the social environment before the move to Saadiyat.

00:44:20–00:46:20 — Campus housing and domestic workers. Brelvi discusses how the design of residential housing affected domestic workers, comparing accommodations at Sama Tower and the Saadiyat campus and reflecting on broader questions of housing and employment.

00:58:30–01:08:30 — Advocacy for domestic workers. She explains how her advocacy developed, discussing research into employment practices, outreach to faculty and staff, and efforts to improve awareness of domestic workers' rights and working conditions.

01:08:30–01:15:30 — Information sessions and community education. Brelvi describes organizing educational sessions and developing resources to help members of the NYUAD community understand ethical employment practices and legal responsibilities when hiring domestic workers.

01:15:30–01:21:30 — Building community through advocacy. She reflects on treating domestic workers as members of the broader NYUAD community and discusses the importance of inclusion, respect, and mutual support.

01:25:00–01:29:30 — Institutionalizing the program. Brelvi explains her efforts to incorporate domestic worker education into HR orientation and collaborate with the Office of Social Responsibility so that programming would continue beyond her own involvement.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Sana Odeh (NYUAD Hackathon) and Lauren Kata

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session, with Sada Odeh, Computer Science Professor of NYU and Chair of NYUAD International Hackathon for Social Good recorded on 2024-06-11and facilitated by Lauren Kata, Associate Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives. Note: the interview was conducated via Zoom, while Dr. Odeh was at a local restaurant.

In this oral history interview, Dr. Sana Odeh reflects on her academic career and leadership at New York University Abu Dhabi, with particular emphasis on founding and developing the NYUAD International Hackathon for Social Good. She discusses the origins of the hackathon, its evolution into a globally recognized interdisciplinary program, and its impact on students, mentors, and collaborators. Odeh describes her commitment to expanding opportunities for students from the Arab world and other underrepresented regions through mentorship, international collaboration, and experiential learning. She recounts how hackathon participants developed projects that evolved into graduate research, entrepreneurial ventures, and academic careers, illustrating the program's long-term impact on students and mentors. Throughout the interview, she reflects on the importance of diversity, cross-cultural collaboration, and challenging stereotypes by bringing together participants from different disciplines, nationalities, and backgrounds. The interview also explores her educational philosophy and her vision for technology as a means of advancing social good and global cooperation.

did.unittitle: Selected index of segments

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: 00:13:50–00:14:34 — Mentorship and professional networks. Dr. Sana Odeh discusses how hackathon participants establish lasting mentoring relationships with industry professionals, recounting the experience of a participant whose mentor at Google (later Facebook) continued to support his academic and professional development.

00:16:56–00:17:40 — Student success and long-term impact. Dr. Odeh recounts how a student developed a healthcare application during the hackathon that later evolved into a capstone project, graduate research, a Rhodes Scholarship, and ultimately a faculty position, illustrating the hackathon's influence on students' academic and professional trajectories.

00:35:07–00:36:02 — Technology for social good. Dr. Odeh explains the philosophy behind organizing the hackathon around broad societal challenges—including healthcare, education, refugees, equality, and sustainability—allowing participants to define meaningful problems and develop interdisciplinary solutions.

01:09:01–01:10:25 — International mentorship and collaboration. Dr. Odeh discusses the role of mentors from organizations such as AstraZeneca and Google, emphasizing how the hackathon provides students, particularly those from underserved regions, with access to global expertise and professional networks.

01:17:13–01:17:30 — Educational outcomes. Dr. Odeh reflects on how hackathon projects frequently develop into capstone projects, research initiatives, and other long-term academic endeavors, highlighting the program's enduring educational impact.

did.unittitle: Interview session with Joseph "Yosi" Gelfand (Physics Program) and Lauren Kata

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Video-recorded session with Dr. Joseph Yosi Gelfand, Associate Professor of Physics at NYU Abu Dhabi, recorded on 2024-06-24 and facilitated by Lauren Kata, Associate Academic Librarian for Archives and Special Collections. Conducted as part of The Memory Project. The Memory Project is the NYUAD Library's contribution to celebrating the memories, experiences, traditions, stories and community history of NYU Abu Dhabi. Interviews will be preserved as part of the NYUAD University Archives.

In this oral history interview, Joseph "Yosi" Gelfand reflects on his career as a professor of physics and observational astronomer and his experiences as a founding faculty member at New York University Abu Dhabi. He discusses the University's early years, describing the academic freedom afforded to faculty as they developed new curricula, including the Foundations of Science program, and helped shape NYUAD's academic culture. Gelfand recalls the challenges of teaching and conducting research across multiple locations before the opening of the Saadiyat campus, reflecting on daily travel between the Downtown Campus, Sama Tower, and the Center for Science and Engineering in Musaffah. He also discusses his research at the Center for Astrophysics and Space Science and the growth of scientific research at NYUAD.
